definition: The hero required for a Team-Up to become active. The anchor usually receives a stat bonus while enabling one or more allies to gain Team-Up effects.
coachNote: Check anchor requirements before building around a Team-Up. If the anchor swaps, the Team-Up plan disappears.
example: Doctor Strange can anchor Arcane Order for Scarlet Witch and Magik.

definition: A hero who benefits from an active Team-Up when the Anchor Hero is present, often gaining a new active or passive Team-Up ability.
coachNote: Secondary Heroes are the payoff pieces. Pick them when your team is already committed to the anchor.
example: Scarlet Witch and Magik are Secondary Heroes in Arcane Order.

definition: A Marvel Rivals mechanic where specific heroes combine powers to grant buffs, extra abilities, or passive effects.
coachNote: Treat Team-Ups as draft incentives, not automatic win conditions. The comp still needs frontline, damage, healing, and map control.
example: Planet X Pals lets Rocket Raccoon and Jeff the Land Shark ride on Groot.

definition: A close-range teamfight style built around durable heroes, sustain, and forcing the enemy to fight in your effective range.
coachNote: Brawl comps want short paths, cover, and committed pushes. Do not slowly walk through long poke sightlines.
example: Hulk, The Thing, Wolverine, and Rogue are examples of heroes that can support brawl plans.

definition: A narrow map area where defenders can concentrate damage and abilities into limited space.
coachNote: Do not trickle through a choke. Rotate, use cover, force cooldowns, or push together with defensive resources.
example: A doorway into an objective room can become a choke if the defending team controls every exit angle.

definition: Abilities that limit enemy movement, interrupt actions, prevent ability use, or otherwise disrupt control of a hero.
coachNote: Track crowd-control cooldowns before diving or using an ultimate that can be interrupted.
example: Stuns, immobilizes, knock downs, and charms are common crowd-control effects.

definition: A high-mobility attack where heroes collapse onto a single target, often a Strategist, then escape or reset before the enemy team can punish.
coachNote: Call the target before diving. One hero jumping alone is usually feeding; two or three jumping together is pressure.
example: Spider-Man, Black Panther, Magik, and Daredevil are examples of dive-friendly heroes.

definition: Attacking from the side or behind, usually bypassing the Vanguard line to pressure Strategists or long-range damage heroes.
coachNote: A flank is strongest when your frontline is already pulling attention. If nobody is looking at your team, they will all look at you.
example: A Duelist wrapping behind the fight to threaten supports is flanking.

definition: A hero who can stay airborne for extended periods or indefinitely, changing how teams must control vertical space.
coachNote: Against flyers, assign hitscan pressure or force objectives where vertical angles are less valuable.
example: Iron Man, Storm, Ultron, and Angela are examples of heroes with sustained flight.

definition: A ranged attack that reaches its target immediately when fired instead of traveling as a projectile.
coachNote: Hitscan pressure is valuable into flyers and fast flankers because there is no projectile travel time to lead.
example: The wiki lists examples such as Hela, Phoenix, and Ultron attacks.

definition: Helping an ally who is under attack, usually by damaging, blocking, healing, crowd-controlling, or bodyguarding the threat.
coachNote: Peel is not only a support job. Vanguards and Duelists should turn when the backline is being collapsed on.
example: Using a stun or shield to save Luna Snow from Spider-Man is peeling.

definition: Dealing damage from distance, ideally from outside the enemy team's effective range, to make engagement safer for the attacker.
coachNote: Poke wins by patience and angle control. It loses value when forced into cramped close-range fights.
example: Hela, Phoenix, Hawkeye, Iron Man, Black Widow, Moon Knight, and Squirrel Girl are listed as poke examples.

definition: A hero's ability to stay alive through health, self-healing, shields, damage reduction, or repeated support.
coachNote: High sustain lets a team hold space longer, but burst damage or anti-heal timing can still break it.
example: A Vanguard with high health and defensive cooldowns has strong sustain.

definition: A common player term used interchangeably with Vanguard.
coachNote: When someone asks for a tank, they usually mean a Vanguard who can start fights, protect space, or absorb pressure.
example: Hulk, Groot, and Doctor Strange are Vanguards.

definition: A common player term used interchangeably with Strategist.
coachNote: Support does not only mean healing. Strategists often bring tempo tools, control, damage boosts, and fight-saving ultimates.
example: Luna Snow, Mantis, and Rocket Raccoon are Strategists.

definition: Damage per second; commonly used by players as another word for Duelist.
coachNote: DPS picks still need a job: poke, dive, flank, brawl, anti-air, or objective pressure.
example: Hela, Spider-Man, and The Punisher are Duelists.

definition: The safer rear area of a team formation where Strategists and long-range heroes often play.
coachNote: Backline players should keep escape routes and line of sight to allies. Frontline players should notice when the backline is pressured.
example: A Luna Snow positioned behind Groot and Punisher is playing in the backline.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals role terminology

definition: The forward pressure line where Vanguards and close-range heroes contest space, block routes, and start engagements.
coachNote: Frontline is about space, not just standing first. Good frontline play creates safe angles for the rest of the team.
example: A Groot walling a doorway while allies rotate is controlling the frontline.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals role terminology

definition: A side angle that pressures the enemy from somewhere other than the main team position without fully committing to a deep flank.
coachNote: Use off-angles to split attention, then leave before the enemy can isolate you.
example: Hela taking a side balcony while her team pushes main is playing an off-angle.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals positioning terms

definition: An elevated position that improves sightlines, cover options, and pressure over enemies below.
coachNote: High ground is valuable until the objective or enemy rotation makes it irrelevant. Do not stay up there after the fight moves away.
example: A Punisher holding a balcony above a choke can pressure enemies before they reach point.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals map-control terms

definition: A map location that gives a player or team multiple options to pressure objective, control lanes, scout enemy movement, and leave safely.
coachNote: Do not treat a power position as a fixed camping spot. Use it to create passive pressure, force enemy cooldowns, then rotate or drop when the fight asks for it.
example: A central high-ground room that sees objective and connects to both side lanes can be a power position.

definition: The ability to influence which routes, sightlines, and side paths the enemy can safely use during a fight.
coachNote: Think in main lane and side lanes. Strong teams do not only walk the shortest route; they open side pressure and make enemies answer multiple directions.
example: Taking a power position that sees main and a side doorway gives your team lane control without standing on point.

definition: Value created by a strong position before committing abilities, because the enemy must respect your angle, movement options, or threat.
coachNote: Passive pressure is not passive play. You are playing aggressively with position while staying patient with cooldowns and cover.
example: Rocket Raccoon holding an off-angle can force Luna Snow to panic-use a cooldown before Rocket fully commits.

definition: Using cover and distance from a cover edge so the enemy can see and shoot as little of your character model as possible.
coachNote: Being near cover is not enough. If your full body is visible, you are giving the enemy too many chances to hit you.
example: Backing up behind horizontal cover until only your head is visible is hitbox minimization.

definition: Adjusting your angle so the enemy is more exposed from your perspective while you still keep your own cover.
coachNote: Small left-right position changes can turn a nearly hidden enemy into a hittable target without making you wide open.
example: Shifting slightly left around a corner so you can see more of an enemy trying to hide behind cover.

definition: A cover principle where you try to expose yourself to only the angle you are actively fighting instead of several threats at once.
coachNote: If you are shooting one target, it usually makes no sense to be visible to three other enemies. Reposition until cover blocks the extra angles.
example: Moving behind a wall so you can shoot the enemy on the right while the enemy on main cannot see you.

definition: Gathering information about enemy location, movement, and cooldowns before committing damage, mobility, or a full engage.
coachNote: Scout from safety before you peek or dive. Good scouting makes mechanics easier because you are not surprised by the target's movement.
example: A Black Widow checking where Spider-Man is moving before charging and peeking for the shot.

definition: A peek where you move sideways along the cover edge instead of walking forward into the enemy sightline.
coachNote: Parallel peeking makes you harder to hit and gives you a cleaner return path back behind cover.
example: Peeking left from a wall, firing, then strafing back behind the same wall instead of walking toward the enemy.

definition: An exchange where one team's position or pressure forces enemy abilities, creating a window to punish with your own cooldowns afterward.
coachNote: Power positions are strong because they can make enemies spend resources first. Survive the response, then punish while their tools are missing.
example: A Strategist angle forces enemy mobility and a defensive cooldown, then your Vanguard engages while those tools are down.

definition: Map geometry or objects that block line of sight, reduce visible hitbox, and create a safe reset point during pressure trades.
coachNote: Cover is your default state, not a panic button. Peek with a purpose, then return before the enemy gets repeated shots.
example: A wall, payload corner, high-ground ledge, doorway, or box can all function as cover depending on the angle.

definition: A line of vision between two points that determines who can see, shoot, heal, or threaten whom.
coachNote: Control sightlines deliberately. A strong sightline pressures enemies; a careless one exposes you to damage you are not ready to answer.
example: Holding a balcony that sees the choke creates a sightline enemies must respect before they can reach objective.

definition: Briefly exposing yourself from cover to gather information, apply pressure, or take a shot before returning to safety.
coachNote: Peek with a purpose. Wide peeking or walking forward into the enemy sightline makes your movement easy to punish.
example: A player shoulder-peeks from a doorway, fires one shot, then returns behind the wall.

definition: The moment a team or player commits into a fight with pressure, mobility, cooldowns, or an ultimate.
coachNote: A good engage has a target, a timing trigger, and an exit or follow-up plan. Going in alone is usually not an engage; it is a feed.
example: Thor jumping the backline as his Duelists open side angles is an engage.

definition: The decision of which enemy should be pressured first based on isolation, cooldowns, health, position, and fight win condition.
coachNote: The best target is not always the closest target. Look for enemies who are exposed, low-resource, or already forced to look away.
example: Punishing a Strategist who used mobility early can be better than shooting a full-health Vanguard.

definition: A plan for emphasizing one part of a hero's kit through team composition, playstyle, cooldown priority, positioning, and fight timing.
coachNote: Marvel Rivals does not use item builds like a MOBA. A build is usually a coaching plan: what ability gets protected, what cooldown starts fights, what stat matters, and what teammates make that plan stronger.
example: Building Rocket Raccoon around revive, armor packs, and lane support is different from building him around damage uptime.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A build plan that prioritizes a hero's non-damage fight tools: crowd control, revive, shields, walls, cleanse-like saves, scouting, disruption, damage boosts, or cooldown denial.
coachNote: A utility build asks, 'What tool wins the fight if I keep it for the right moment?' You play around timing, line of sight, and cooldown discipline instead of spending every ability for raw damage.
example: An Adam Warlock utility build protects Karmic Revival and Soul Bond timing so the team can survive burst and re-engage.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A build plan centered on maximizing damage uptime, burst windows, target pressure, and finishing power.
coachNote: Damage builds still need setup. The mistake is treating damage as constant spam instead of pairing it with angles, forced cooldowns, and target priority.
example: A Hela damage build plays for clean sightlines, off-angles, and burst timing after enemy defensive tools are spent.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A build plan that emphasizes staying power through healing, shields, armor, damage reduction, revive value, or repeated defensive resources.
coachNote: Sustain builds are strongest when they help your team hold or retake space. They lose value if the team only survives without creating pressure.
example: A Groot sustain build focuses on walls, body blocking, and cover cycling so allies can stabilize and keep fighting.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A build plan centered on movement tools, rotations, chase routes, escape paths, vertical access, and fast target swaps.
coachNote: Mobility builds are not just about moving more. They are about arriving first, leaving safely, and forcing enemies to turn at the wrong time.
example: A Spider-Man mobility build uses web movement to scout, isolate a backline target, and reset before peel arrives.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A build plan focused on charging, protecting, and converting a hero's ultimate into a fight-winning timing window.
coachNote: Ultimate builds should not hold the whole team hostage. Use regular cooldowns to force enemy answers first, then spend the ultimate when follow-up is ready.
example: A Luna Snow ultimate build plays safe before the enemy engage, then uses Fate of Both Worlds to swing the committed fight.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A ranked-ladder distortion where smurfs or alternate accounts temporarily add high-skill players into lower visible ranks, making those ranks feel harder or more populated with strong players than they really are.
coachNote: Do not read every hard lobby as proof you belong lower. Smurfs can make a rank feel uneven because the account rank and the player's real skill do not match.
example: A Diamond-level player on a fresh Gold account can inflate Gold by playing far above the lobby's expected skill while climbing through it.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals ranked discussion

definition: Good game. Usually said after a match ends.
coachNote: Good game.
example: gg team

definition: Good luck, have fun. A friendly phrase used at the start of a match.
coachNote: Good luck, have fun.
example: glhf everyone

definition: Laughing out loud. Used to show something is funny or unserious.
coachNote: Laughing out loud.
example: lol that was wild

definition: Laughing out loud, I guess. A casual phrase used to show mild amusement, uncertainty, awkward agreement, or reluctant acceptance.
coachNote: Laughing out loud, I guess.
example: lol ig that works

definition: Be right back. Used when someone steps away briefly.
coachNote: Be right back.
example: brb one sec

definition: Away from keyboard. The player is not currently active.
coachNote: Away from keyboard.
example: our healer is afk

definition: Nice try. Used to encourage someone after a failed play or close round.
coachNote: Nice try.
example: nt, we almost had it

definition: Well played. A compliment for a good move, round, or match.
coachNote: Well played.
example: wp on point

definition: Short for difference. Often used to say one player or role outperformed the other side.
coachNote: A performance gap between players or roles.
example: tank diff

definition: Intentionally feeding, or playing so recklessly that it looks intentional.
coachNote: Playing recklessly or intentionally feeding.
example: stop inting mid

definition: To die repeatedly or give the enemy easy value, especially when those deaths charge enemy ultimates or create unfair fights.
coachNote: Feeding is often a positioning or timing problem, not just a mechanics problem. Reset, group, and stop entering fights before the team is ready.
example: Do not feed before our supports get back from spawn.

definition: Playing in a way that causes your team to lose, either intentionally or through very reckless decisions.
coachNote: Use this carefully. A bad play is not always throwing; look for repeated choices that ignore the team's win condition.
example: stop throwing and group before the next fight

definition: Manipulating someone into doubting what they saw, remembered, or understood. In game chat, it is often used when someone denies obvious mistakes or tries to rewrite what happened.
coachNote: Use this term carefully. Not every disagreement or bad call is gas-lighting; look for repeated denial or blame-shifting that makes teammates question reality.
example: Saying the team never grouped after everyone clearly waited together can feel like gas-lighting.

definition: Frustrated enough that emotions are hurting decision-making.
coachNote: Frustrated and playing worse because of it.
example: I am tilted after that loss

definition: The currently favored strategies, characters, builds, or tactics in a game.
coachNote: What is currently considered strong or optimal.
example: that comp is meta

definition: A balance change that makes a character, weapon, ability, or strategy weaker.
coachNote: A change that makes something weaker.
example: they nerfed that hero

definition: A balance change or effect that makes a character, weapon, ability, or strategy stronger.
coachNote: A change or effect that makes something stronger.
example: the patch buffed tanks

definition: To ambush an enemy, usually with help from another player or from an unexpected angle.
coachNote: An ambush on an enemy player.
example: watch for the gank

definition: To protect a teammate by pushing away, controlling, or damaging enemies who are attacking them.
coachNote: Protecting a teammate from pressure.
example: peel for supports

definition: Short for ultimate ability, usually a powerful ability charged over time.
coachNote: Ultimate ability.
example: save ult for next fight

definition: Cooldown. The time before an ability or item can be used again.
coachNote: Cooldown.
example: heal is on cd

definition: Area of effect. Describes damage, healing, or control that affects an area instead of one target.
coachNote: Area of effect.
example: avoid the aoe

definition: Crowd control. Effects that limit enemy movement or actions, such as stuns, slows, or knockbacks.
coachNote: Crowd control.
example: save cc for their ult

term: #wood players need cas to get dope
definition: Likely a trash-talk phrase aimed at lower-skill players. In context, it probably means "low-rank players need casual matches, carry help, or easy lobbies to get good rewards or look good." The wording is not standard, so treat it as heard-in-game slang rather than a fixed gaming term.
coachNote: Likely trash talk about lower-skill players needing easy help to get good results.
example: #wood players need cas to get dope

definition: A likely insult for very low-rank or low-skill players. It is similar to calling someone "bronze" or "low elo," but with "wood" implying a rank below normal competitive tiers.
coachNote: Trash talk for very low-rank or low-skill players.
example: wood players need help

definition: Usually short for casual or casuals, depending on context. In game chat it can mean casual matches, casual players, or non-ranked play.
coachNote: Usually casual or casuals.
example: play cas first

definition: Slang for something good, cool, impressive, or desirable. In gaming chat it may describe rewards, cosmetics, plays, or gear.
coachNote: Cool, good, impressive, or desirable.
example: that skin is dope

definition: A position or line of attack that changes who can see, shoot, heal, or threaten whom. In Marvel Rivals coaching, an angle is usually about creating pressure from a useful direction while still having cover or an escape path.
coachNote: A position or line of attack that creates pressure from a useful direction.
example: Taking the balcony angle lets Hela pressure the enemy backline while her team walks main.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals positioning terms

definition: The Marvel Rivals frontline role. Vanguards usually have larger health pools, defensive tools, and space-making abilities that let a team start or survive fights.
coachNote: A Vanguard's job is not only to soak damage. Good Vanguard play creates timing, cover, and pressure so allies can safely deal damage or heal.
example: Doctor Strange, Groot, and Venom are Vanguards.

definition: The Marvel Rivals damage role. Duelists focus on pressure, burst, finishing kills, flanking, or controlling sightlines through damage threat.
coachNote: Duelist does not mean ignore the team. Pick a damage job: break shields, pressure flyers, finish isolated targets, or open an angle for the next engage.
example: Hela, Spider-Man, and The Punisher are Duelists.

definition: The Marvel Rivals support role. Strategists help teammates through healing, buffs, utility, damage support, and fight-saving cooldowns.
coachNote: Strategists are not heal bots. Their cooldown timing, survival, and utility often decide whether a team can reset, re-engage, or survive an ultimate.
example: Luna Snow, Mantis, and Rocket Raccoon are Strategists.

definition: An objective mode where the attacking team escorts a moving objective through the map while defenders try to stop or reverse its progress.
coachNote: Convoy is about progress management. Attackers need clean pushes around checkpoints; defenders need staged contests and retakes instead of late trickles.
example: On Convoy, attackers should group before pushing the vehicle through a dangerous choke.

definition: A control-point mode where both teams fight over a single objective zone and win the round by holding it to the required score.
coachNote: Domination rewards clean rotations and retakes. Do not spend ultimates after the point is already lost unless the team can actually flip it.
example: A team may win Domination by capturing two objective rounds before the other team does.

definition: A hybrid objective mode where attackers first capture a checkpoint, then escort a moving objective through later checkpoints if the first capture succeeds.
coachNote: Convergence asks for two plans: how to break the first point, then how to manage the escort phase once the map opens up.
example: After winning the first checkpoint in Convergence, the attacking team swaps from point pressure into escort path control.

definition: An arcade mode where eliminations drop points that enemies can collect, while allies can pick them up to deny the score.
coachNote: In Conquest, confirming the point drop matters as much as getting the elimination. Do not leave free points on the floor after a pick.
example: After eliminating an enemy in Conquest, step forward only if it is safe enough to collect or deny the dropped point.

definition: A free-for-all arcade mode where players compete for Final Hits, with winners determined by the top half of the lobby or the target score.
coachNote: Doom Match is about finishing power and survival. Chasing every duel can hand Final Hits to third parties.
example: In Doom Match, a low-health escape can be better than forcing a duel that another player can clean up.

definition: A mode where teams fight over resource zones, drain resources from captured areas, and may unlock a final contest point depending on progress.
coachNote: Resource Rumble is won by rotating early and controlling the next zone before the resource timer forces everyone there.
example: When the first resource zone is nearly depleted, rotate toward the next likely fight instead of overcommitting late.

definition: A competitive draft rule where teams remove selected heroes from the match so neither side can play them.
coachNote: Ban heroes that break your planned composition, dominate the current map, or hard-counter your team's best win condition.
example: A team might ban a strong flyer if its composition has weak anti-air pressure.
sourceName: TechRadar - Marvel Rivals ranks and Competitive Mode

definition: A ranked protection mechanic that can prevent a demotion or point loss after enough losses have charged it.
coachNote: Chrono Shield is a buffer, not a strategy. If it triggers, use the saved rank as a cue to review what is causing the losing streak.
example: A fully charged Chrono Shield can shatter instead of letting a ranked loss demote you.
sourceName: TechRadar - Marvel Rivals ranks and Competitive Mode

definition: A ranked match used near the start of a season to help determine a player's starting rank around a projected skill level.
coachNote: Play placements for stable habits, not desperation. A small sample rewards consistency more than risky hero swaps every fight.
example: After a new season begins, placement matches help decide where a player starts on the ranked ladder.

definition: Map geometry that can be damaged or broken, changing sightlines, cover, routes, and fight positions during a match.
coachNote: Use destruction deliberately. Breaking cover can expose enemies, but it can also remove your own reset point if you are careless.
example: Destroying a wall can open a new sightline onto an objective or deny an enemy's safe corner.

definition: Progress toward a hero's ultimate ability, built over time and through combat actions until the ultimate becomes available.
coachNote: Track ultimate charge by fight rhythm. If the enemy used several high-value cooldowns and won a long fight, expect ultimates soon.
example: Feeding before a regroup can give enemies extra ultimate charge for the next fight.

definition: The finishing blow that secures an elimination credit, especially important in modes that score or rank players by final hits.
coachNote: Final Hits matter, but do not tunnel on them. Pressuring the right target can win a fight even if someone else gets the final blow.
example: Doom Match rewards players for securing Final Hits before the match ends.

definition: Credit for helping an ally secure an elimination through support actions such as healing, slowing, boosting, or other utility.
coachNote: In Marvel Rivals, assists reward meaningful support value. Strategists can contribute heavily without being the final damage source.
example: Healing an ally while they finish a target can contribute to an assist.
sourceName: Polygon - How to get assists in Marvel Rivals

definition: Accidentally leaving an objective during a capture, stall, or overtime moment and losing progress or the point because nobody is contesting it.
coachNote: Before chasing kills, check whether someone is still touching objective. Many lost fights are really lost objective discipline.
example: The team got three eliminations but C9'd because nobody stayed on point.

definition: Short for disconnected. A player has lost connection to the match or left unexpectedly.
coachNote: If a teammate DCs, play for delay and survivability until the match system or teammate reconnect decides what happens next.
example: Our Vanguard DC'd before the second fight.

definition: A player rating concept used as shorthand for ranked skill or matchmaking strength. In Marvel Rivals discussion, players use it to describe rank movement and match quality.
coachNote: Do not judge improvement only by Elo after one session. Look for repeated decisions that make your next ten games more stable.
example: A player might say they are in low Elo when their ranked matches feel less coordinated.

definition: A map hazard or drop-off that can instantly eliminate players who fall into it, often making displacement abilities more dangerous nearby.
coachNote: Fight differently near pits. Save mobility, respect knockbacks, and avoid standing where one displacement turns a won fight into a lost one.
example: Spider-Man can threaten pit kills by displacing enemies near a ledge.

definition: A balance update that changes how a hero, ability, or mechanic works, often mixing buffs and nerfs rather than simply making something stronger or weaker.
coachNote: After a rebalance, reread the kit instead of relying on old muscle memory. The hero's job may have shifted even if the name stayed the same.
example: A patch can rebalance an ability by lowering its burst damage but adding more charges.

definition: Player versus environment. A mode type where players fight non-player enemies rather than another player team.
coachNote: PvE is useful for learning basic aim, movement, and ability flow, but it will not teach every habit needed against real players.
example: Practice vs. AI is a PvE-style option.

definition: Player versus player. A mode type where teams or players fight against other human-controlled opponents.
coachNote: PvP punishes predictable movement and poor timing faster than PvE. Treat scouting and cooldown discipline as part of the fundamentals.
example: Quick Match and Competitive are PvP modes.

definition: When a highly experienced or high-ranked player uses a lower-ranked or new account, often creating uneven matches for less experienced players.
coachNote: A smurf can distort one match, but do not let it distort your review. Separate unwinnable pressure from mistakes you can actually fix.
example: A high-rank Duelist on a new account can make a low-rank lobby feel much harder than normal.

definition: A hero-specific progression system where players complete challenges and earn Proficiency Points to unlock ranks and cosmetic rewards.
coachNote: Proficiency shows time and task completion on a hero, not guaranteed decision quality. Use it as motivation, then still review fight choices.
example: A player can raise Luna Snow proficiency by completing hero challenges over many matches.

definition: A buff that increases the amount of damage a hero deals while the effect is active.
coachNote: Damage boost is strongest when paired with timing. Boost a planned burst window, not random poke that the enemy can safely heal.
example: A Strategist damage boost can help a Duelist finish an exposed target faster.

definition: A buff that reduces incoming damage while active, helping a hero survive pressure or commit into danger.
coachNote: Damage reduction is a timing tool. Use it to cross space, survive enemy burst, or hold a position long enough for help to arrive.
example: A Vanguard may use damage reduction before leading the team through a choke.

definition: A debuff that reduces the healing a hero receives, making sustain and support resources less effective during the effect.
coachNote: When healing reduction lands, swap from slow pressure to confirmation. The window is valuable because normal sustain cannot fully answer it.
example: A healing reduction effect can make a high-sustain Vanguard much easier to finish.

definition: A cleanse effect that removes active debuffs from a hero.
coachNote: Purify effects are fight-saving when held for the right debuff. Do not spend them early if the enemy still has a stronger control tool available.
example: Purify can remove a dangerous debuff before an ally is focused down.

definition: A debuff that disables movement abilities such as dashes or jumps, making mobile heroes easier to punish.
coachNote: Earthbound creates a focus-fire window. Call the target immediately because mobility heroes become much easier to finish while grounded.
example: Applying Earthbound to a flyer can stop their escape long enough for the team to punish.

definition: A debuff that prevents a hero from moving until the effect ends, sometimes described as imprisoning the target.
coachNote: Immobilize is strongest when teammates are ready to follow up. A rooted target still needs damage or displacement to become a won fight.
example: Immobilizing a flanker can give the backline time to turn and eliminate them.

definition: A debuff that prevents a hero from using their ultimate ability while the effect is active.
coachNote: Silence effects are strongest against heroes who are waiting to turn the fight with an ultimate. Track likely ultimate windows before spending it.
example: Silencing a hero before they can ult can stop a fight-winning counterplay.

definition: The main non-ranked queue for standard Marvel Rivals objective play, featuring modes such as Domination, Convoy, and Convergence.
coachNote: Use Quick Match to practice heroes and map flow, but still build ranked habits: regroup, track cooldowns, and play the objective.
example: A player can practice a new Strategist in Quick Match before taking them into Competitive.

definition: The ranked Marvel Rivals queue where players earn or lose rank points, climb divisions, and play higher-stakes versions of the core objective modes.
coachNote: Competitive rewards consistency more than hero hopping. Pick a small pool, review repeated mistakes, and manage tilt between matches.
example: Competitive unlocks ranked progression and uses modes such as Domination, Convoy, and Convergence.

definition: A casual hub for alternate Marvel Rivals modes that use different rules from the standard objective queues.
coachNote: Arcade can sharpen mechanics and hero comfort, but always check the mode rules before judging whether a play was correct.
example: Conquest and Doom Match are examples of Arcade-style modes.

definition: A practice queue where players fight AI-controlled opponents using objective rules similar to standard match modes.
coachNote: Practice vs. AI is best for learning controls, ability ranges, and map routes. Move to PvP when you need realistic pressure and punishment.
example: A beginner can use Practice vs. AI to learn how Convoy objectives move.

definition: A private or custom rules match setup where players can choose modes, maps, and settings outside normal matchmaking.
coachNote: Custom games are excellent for map walkthroughs, team drills, and testing hero interactions without match pressure.
example: A team can load a Custom Game to practice a first-point Convergence setup.

definition: A large-team mode where two sides score by earning eliminations, with a target score or timer deciding the winner.
coachNote: In elimination-heavy modes, survival and cleanup matter more than slow objective posture. Avoid feeding points through isolated duels.
example: 18 vs. 18 Annihilation rewards teams for reaching the kill target before time expires.

definition: An Arcade-style mode using Quick Match-style rules while allowing duplicate heroes, with ban rules used to keep compositions from becoming too warped.
coachNote: Free Fight changes composition expectations. Do not assume normal role balance or duplicate-hero limits apply.
example: A Free Fight lobby can include repeated hero picks that standard queues would restrict.

definition: A final contest window that keeps an objective fight alive when the timer or score would otherwise end, usually because a team is still contesting.
coachNote: Overtime is not the moment for perfect setups. Touch safely, survive, and chain resources so the team gets one more real fight.
example: A Vanguard may touch in overtime so teammates can respawn and recontest.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals objective modes

definition: Standing in or near an objective area to prevent the enemy from freely capturing, scoring, or moving the objective.
coachNote: Contest with a plan. A clean touch that buys time is useful; walking in alone with no escape usually just feeds ultimate charge.
example: A mobile hero can contest the point briefly while the team rotates back.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals objective modes

definition: A progress marker in objective modes that attackers try to capture or escort toward, often extending the round or changing the map phase.
coachNote: Play differently around checkpoints. Attackers should save enough resources to finish the last meters; defenders should stage a real retake before progress locks in.
example: Convergence starts with a checkpoint before shifting into an escort phase.

definition: Temporary extra health granted by an ability, buff, or effect, increasing how much damage a hero can survive before being eliminated.
coachNote: Treat bonus health as a timing resource. Push while it is active, then reset before the extra durability disappears.
example: A Vanguard may gain bonus health before committing into the enemy frontline.

definition: A healing effect that restores health gradually for a duration instead of all at once.
coachNote: Healing over time needs patience and cover. If you keep taking burst damage while it ticks, the value disappears before it stabilizes you.
example: A Strategist may apply healing over time before the team re-engages.

definition: A state where a hero is temporarily immune to incoming damage.
coachNote: Do not waste burst into invulnerability. Track when the effect ends and aim to punish the exit timing instead.
example: Some abilities briefly make a hero invulnerable during movement or transformation.

definition: A healing effect that restores health based on a portion of the damage dealt to enemies.
coachNote: Lifesteal rewards uptime, but it still loses to burst or crowd control. Keep hitting only when your position lets you survive the trade.
example: A hero with lifesteal can sustain longer while actively damaging enemies.

definition: A buff that increases movement speed, helping a hero rotate, chase, disengage, or cross dangerous space faster.
coachNote: Movement boost is strongest when the team moves with the timing. Speeding in alone usually turns a good buff into an early feed.
example: A movement boost can help a team cross a long sightline before poke damage stacks up.

definition: A state where a hero becomes invisible or much harder for enemies to see, usually to scout, reposition, or set up an attack.
coachNote: Stealth creates first-look advantage, not guaranteed safety. Use it to choose a better angle and exit path before committing.
example: A stealth hero can scout a backline route before starting a flank.

definition: The top Marvel Rivals ranked designation, commonly shortened to OAA, used for the highest players at the top of the competitive ladder.
coachNote: One Above All is a leaderboard goal, not a normal improvement benchmark. Review decisions by fight quality, then let rank follow the consistency.
example: A player chasing One Above All needs strong mechanics, hero mastery, map knowledge, and long-term ranked consistency.

definition: Short for One Above All, the highest Marvel Rivals ranked designation for top competitive players.
coachNote: When players say OAA, they usually mean the very top end of the ladder rather than simply a strong ranked player.
example: That Duelist peaked OAA last season.

definition: A very high Marvel Rivals rank below the One Above All leaderboard tier, where players continue accumulating points instead of moving through normal sub-tiers.
coachNote: At Eternity, small mistakes get punished quickly. Prioritize stable hero pools, matchup knowledge, and clean mental resets between games.
example: An Eternity player is expected to understand rotations, ultimate economy, and draft pressure at a high level.

definition: A high Marvel Rivals competitive rank below Eternity and above the lower ladder ranks.
coachNote: Celestial games often demand strong role discipline. Winning usually comes from coordinated timings, not just individual outplays.
example: A Celestial support should know when to kite, peel, and save cooldowns for enemy ultimates.

definition: A high Marvel Rivals competitive rank below Celestial and above Diamond.
coachNote: Grandmaster games usually punish loose ultimates, late rotations, and unclear target calls. Climbing here often means tightening teamfight planning.
example: A Grandmaster player should understand when to save resources for the next objective fight instead of chasing stagger kills.

definition: A Marvel Rivals competitive rank above Platinum and below Grandmaster.
coachNote: Diamond is where good mechanics need cleaner decisions behind them. Track ultimates, rotate with purpose, and avoid autopilot engages.
example: A Diamond team can lose a winnable match by staggering before every objective fight.

definition: A Marvel Rivals competitive rank above Gold and below higher ladder ranks such as Diamond and Celestial.
coachNote: Platinum is where repeated fundamentals matter more: fewer stagger deaths, cleaner ultimate plans, and better adaptation to enemy win conditions.
example: A Platinum player may climb faster by reducing solo engages and improving target priority.

definition: A middle Marvel Rivals competitive rank above Silver and below Platinum.
coachNote: Gold is a good place to tighten one or two core habits at a time: group before fights, use cover, and stop spending ultimates after fights are already lost.
example: A Gold player who learns to reset instead of feeding can climb without changing heroes.

definition: A Marvel Rivals competitive rank above Bronze and below Gold.
coachNote: Silver improvement usually starts with fight structure: regroup, identify the objective, and use abilities with a reason instead of on cooldown.
example: A Silver team can win more fights by waiting for all six players before re-engaging.

definition: One of the starting lower ranks in Marvel Rivals Competitive, below Silver.
coachNote: Bronze climbing is mostly fundamentals: stay near cover, learn your hero's job, group before fights, and avoid giving away free deaths.
example: A Bronze player can improve quickly by focusing on survival and simple team timing.
